title: A.M. v. I.V.
text:
2026 BCSC 471 A.M. v. I.V.
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF BRITISH COLUMBIA
Citation:
A.M. v. I.V.,
2026 BCSC 471
Date: 20260319
Docket: E60220
Registry: New Westminster
Between:
A.M.
Claimant
And
I.V.
Respondent
Before: The Honourable Justice Norell
Reasons for Judgment
The Claimant, appearing in person:
A.M.
The Respondent, appearing in person:
I.V.
Place and Date of Hearing:
New Westminster, B.C.
January 16, 2026
Place and Date of Judgment:
New Westminster, B.C.
March 19, 2026
Introduction
[1] These are reasons on the claimant's application to determine the following:
a) Issue 1: What are the parties' incomes for 2023 and 2024 pursuant to the Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175 [Guidelines]?
b) Issue 2: What is the amount of table monthly child support owing from July 1, 2024, to date?
c) Issue 3: Which expenses claimed by each party are shareable expenses under s. 7 of the Guidelines, and what is the amount owing by each party?
Background
[2] The parties married in 2017, separated in 2019, and divorced in 2022. They have one child, K, who is 8 years old. The parties share parenting time equally.
[3] The claimant is a drafter and self-employed through his own corporation (the "Claimant Corporation"). The respondent is a bookkeeper and is employed by a business. She also has her own part-time bookkeeping corporation (the "Respondent Corporation") which generates a little income.
[4] On May 29, 2023, the parties entered into a final order settling all matters between them (the "Consent Order"). The portions of the Consent Order relevant to each issue are discussed under that heading.
Issue 1: What are the parties' Guidelines incomes for 2023 and 2024?
Legal Framework
[5] The Guidelines provide the framework for determining income for the purpose of child support. The relevant provisions are set out in ss. 15 to 20. Pursuant to s. 16, the calculation commences with a spouse's total income from line 15000 of their personal tax return and is adjusted in accordance with Schedule III of the Guidelines: Brown v. Brown, 2014 BCCA 152 at para. 16; Vincent v. Vincent, 2012 BCCA 186 at para. 35. The court must then consider if the circumstances in ss. 17 to 20 are present and whether there should be an adjustment to arrive at the fairest determination of income available for support.
[6] The portions of Schedule III of the Guidelines which are relevant to the parties' arguments are:
s. 5 Replace the taxable amount of dividends from taxable Canadian corporations received by the spouse by the actual amount of those dividends received by the spouse.
s. 7 Deduct the actual amount of business investment losses suffered by the spouse during the year.
s. 9 Where the spouse's net self-employment income is determined by deducting an amount for salaries, benefits, wages or management fees, or other payments, paid to or on behalf of persons with whom the spouse does not deal at arm's length, include that amount, unless the spouse establishes that the payments were necessary to earn the self-employment income and were reasonable in the circumstances.
[7] Section 17 concerns situations where a spouse's income fluctuates or there has been a non-recurring receipt of income, or a non-recurring capital or business investment loss. Section 17 states:
17 (1) If the court is of the opinion that the determination of a spouse's annual income under section 16 would not be the fairest determination of that income, the court may have regard to the spouse's income over the last three years and determine an amount that is fair and reasonable in light of any pattern of income, fluctuation in income or receipt of a non-recurring amount during those years.
(2) Where a spouse has incurred a non-recurring capital or business investment loss, the court may, if it is of the opinion that the determination of the spouse's annual income under section 16 would not provide the fairest determination of the annual income, choose not to apply sections 6 and 7 of Schedule III, and adjust the amount of the loss, including related expenses and carrying charges and interest expenses, to arrive at such amount as the court considers appropriate.
[8] If a court determines that a spouse's annual income under s. 16 would not be the fairest determination of income, s. 17 permits the court to determine an amount of income that is fair and reasonable, for example by averaging income over three years: Harras v. Lhotka, 2016 BCCA 246 at paras. 22-35. A court may also not include all of a non-recurring receipt of an amount or may not deduct all of a non-recurring capital or business investment loss.
[9] Section 18 concerns situations where the spouse is a shareholder, director, or officer of a corporation. Section 18 states:
18 (1) Where a spouse is a shareholder, director or officer of a corporation and the court is of the opinion that the amount of the spouse's annual income as determined under section 16 does not fairly reflect all the money available to the spouse for the payment of child support, the court may consider the situations described in section 17 and determine the spouse's annual income to include
(a) all or part of the pre-tax income of the corporation, and of any corporation that is related to that corporation, for the most recent taxation year; or
(b) an amount commensurate with the services that the spouse provides to the corporation, provided that the amount does not exceed the corporation's pre-tax income.
(2) In determining the pre-tax income of a corporation for the purposes of subsection (1), all amounts paid by the corporation as salaries, wages or management fees, or other payments or benefits, to or on behalf of persons with whom the corporation does not deal at arm's length must be added to the pre-tax income, unless the spouse establishes that the payments were reasonable in the circumstances.
[10] Section 18(1) permits the court to include some or all of the corporation's pre-tax income in a spouse's income if it concludes that s. 16 does not fairly reflect all the money available to the spouse for the payment of support: Hausmann v. Klukas, 2009 BCCA 32 at paras. 32. The onus is on the payor to establish that the corporation's pre-tax income is not available to the payor: Hausman at paras. 51-52. Section 18(2) requires a court to add back salaries or wages paid to a non-arm's length person by the corporation unless the spouse establishes that the payments were reasonable in the circumstances.
[11] Section 19 permits a court to impute income to a spouse in certain circumstances. The portions of s. 19 relevant to the parties' arguments are:
(1) The court may impute such amount of income to a spouse as it considers appropriate in the circumstances, which circumstances include the following:
(a) the spouse is intentionally under-employed or unemployed, other than where the under-employment or unemployment is required by the needs of a child of the marriage or any child under the age of majority or by the reasonable educational or health needs of the spouse;
(f) the spouse has failed to provide income information when under a legal obligation to do so;
(g) the spouse unreasonably deducts expenses from income;
(2) For the purpose of paragraph (1)(g), the reasonableness of an expense deduction is not solely governed by whether the deduction is permitted under the Income Tax Act.
[12] With respect to s. 19(1)(a), it is not necessary for the applicant to show that there was an intent to evade or minimize child support; what matters is whether the parent is earning what the parent is capable of earning: Barker v. Barker, 2005 BCCA 177 at paras. 18-19. In determining whether a party is intentionally underemployed, the Court must consider whether the spouse has taken reasonable steps to obtain employment commensurate with their age, health, education, skills, and work history: Marquez v. Zapiola, 2013 BCCA 433 at para. 37. Parents who are healthy and can work have a duty to seek employment, and persistence in an un-remunerative employment will not be an excuse: Fong v. Fong, 2011 BCSC 42 at para. 82. The onus is on the person seeking to impute income to show an evidentiary basis on a balance of probabilities: Marquez at para. 36.
[13] With respect to s. 19(1)(f), s. 21 of the Guidelines sets out the obligations of each spouse with respect to income disclosure. In this case, as there is shared parenting, both parties' incomes must be disclosed. For spouses who are self-employed or control a corporation, s. 21(d) and (f) state that the disclosure must include the financial statements of the business and a statement showing a breakdown of all amounts paid to or on behalf of non-arm's length persons or other corporations.
[14] Finally, with respect to s. 19(1)(g), when a party argues for imputation of income on the basis that the other spouse is unreasonably deducting business expenses, there is a shifting onus of proof. This was explained by Justice Fleming, then of this Court, in Sahi v. Sahi, 2023 BCSC 736:
[286] Although the party seeking to impute income bears the onus of proof, under s. 19(1)(g) of the Guidelines, the initial evidentiary burden is met by showing the other party had deducted business expenses: D.M.B. v. D.W.A.L., 2018 BCSC 1254 at para. 140; and C.M. v. V.M., 2016 BCSC 2144 at paras. 39-40. The onus then shifts to the other party to demonstrate the business expenses are reasonably deducted from income for child support purposes: C.M. This requires the claimed expense to not only be reasonable, but also to have legitimate business purpose. Personal expenses, that is expenses that provide a personal benefit and/or constitute an ordinary living expense, should be added back into the other party's income: D.M.B.; Kowalewich v. Kowalewich, 2001 BCCA 450.
[15] This means that when expenses are challenged, simply providing the year end financial statements, without more, may not be sufficient to demonstrate that business expenses are reasonably deducted from income for the purpose of child support.
Consent Order
[16] The terms of the Consent Order relevant to Guidelines incomes are:
a) For the purpose of calculating support, the claimant's income is $100,000 and the respondent's income is $47,664. Pursuant to s. 9 of the Guidelines, the set off net amount payable for monthly child support is $498 commencing June 1, 2023 (terms 51 and 52); and
b) By May 1 of each year, the parties will exchange financial information in accordance with s. 21 of the Guidelines (term 56) and commencing July 1 of each year the parties will adjust the monthly child support and proportionate sharing of s. 7 special and extraordinary expenses (terms 57 and 58).
Claimant's Income
Parties' Positions
[17] The claimant submits his Guidelines income for 2023 should be $53,597, and for 2024 should be $21,000. Both incomes are primarily dividends taken from the Claimant Corporation.
[18] The claimant states that the $100,000 income imputed to him in the Consent Order was a compromise to reach an all-encompassing settlement, and with the mutual understanding that updated financial disclosure would be shared annually and support adjusted accordingly. He states this figure did not fairly reflect his actual income at the time.
[19] The claimant states that in September 2022, he left his full-time salaried position with an employer (for which he was paid through the Claimant Corporation) to focus on becoming self-employed. This decision was made in good faith with the goal of increasing his long-term earning potential and flexibility as a parent. He has been self-employed full time since then, with his income directly tied to the availability of the Claimant Corporation's contract work.
[20] The Claimant Corporation's 2023 pre-tax net income was $107,167. The claimant submits that this income should not be included in his Guidelines income because the income was "retained in the company and used for employee salaries, contractor payments, and operations expenses necessary to sustain the business." The claimant submits that the respondent's analysis of the Claimant Corporation's financial records is misleading. The bank balance and accounts receivable are not income. Cash flow also fluctuates significantly and cannot be treated as disposable income. It typically takes 45-90 days for clients to pay invoices, while the Claimant Corporation is responsible for paying employees and subcontractors on a bi-weekly basis. Those payments average between $8,000 and $10,000 every two weeks.
[21] The claimant states that wages to his new spouse were for actual administrative work performed during her maternity leave. She organized and scanned receipts for bookkeeping, prepared payroll, renewed business licenses, and followed up on accounts receivable. The claimant disputes that all of some business expenses claimed are actually personal expenses, but acknowledges there is a personal component to those expenses. The claimant works from home. Rent was prorated based on the portion of his home used as an office. Meal expenses include meals taken with employees, clients, or while out of the office. Vehicle expenses include travel for site visits and client meetings. Office supplies include consumables, small tools, and various computer equipment necessary to operate the business. He told the respondent he is agreeable to adding back certain expenses such as meals, vehicle, phone, travel, and personal insurance, provided the same is done for the Respondent Corporation.
[22] The claimant states the Claimant Corporation's current business is:
plummeting and my company is in loss due to a sharp decline in client demand, including the loss of a major client and the cancellation of multiple contracts. As a result, I was forced to reduce my team from seven employees to three and significantly scale back operations. I did not receive any salary or shareholder loan repayments. I am continuing to operate the business in good faith and have been actively working on rebuilding the client base. However, my income for the year was significantly reduced and reflects the company's financial hardship.
[23] The claimant corrected in a later affidavit that he did receive repayment of shareholder loans in 2024. In fact, he both received repayment and borrowed from the Claimant Corporation.
[24] In 2024, the Claimant Corporation had a loss of $31,087. The claimant states he has accumulated $40,000 in personal debt in 2023 and 2024 to cover basic living expenses, parenting costs, and child support payments. He provides a copy of a bank statement supporting this.
[25] The claimant disputes that he has not provided financial disclosure. He has provided the 2023 and 2024 corporate and personal tax returns, which include the Claimant Corporation's profit and loss statement and balance sheet. He was produced the Claimant Corporation's 2023 general ledger, but not the 2024 general ledger.
[26] The respondent seeks to impute $120,000 income to the claimant for 2023 and 2024 on four bases. First, the respondent submits that the claimant's Guidelines income should include all the Claimant Corporation's pre-tax income. The respondent questions the need to keep money within the Claimant Corporation. The respondent disputes that the Claimant Corporation's financial position is as bleak as painted by the claimant. She provides her own analysis of the financial records and submits that they do not support the claimant's claim that there has been a drop in sales. She refers to the increase in the total subcontractor and payroll expenses from 2023 to 2024 and argues that the claimant must not be working.
[27] Second, the respondent submits that the claimant unreasonably deducts expenses. Many of the expenses claimed by the Claimant Corporation are personal expenses. The claimant also employs his new spouse and has not made full disclosure regarding this.
[28] Third, the respondent submits that the claimant is capable of earning more than what he currently claims as his income. The respondent points to the claimant's historical ability to make more, and the agreed amount in the Consent Order. She refers to internet research she has conducted regarding the average salaries of drafters and engineer drafters.
[29] Fourth, the respondent submits that the claimant has failed to make full financial disclosure. The claimant has only recently produced the general ledger for 2023, but no receipts, and the general ledger for 2024 has not been produced.
Analysis
[30] The claimant's 2023 line 15000 income is $51,937, which includes $51,570 in taxable Canadian corporation dividends. Adjusting this in accordance with Schedule III of the Guidelines, the actual amount of the dividends was $45,000, so the adjusted line 15000 income is $45,187.
[31] Turning to the Claimant Corporation's financial statements for 2023, its pre-tax income was $107,167. The total of subcontracted services and employment wages was $155,759 on sales revenue of $286,964. The respondent's analysis refers to the gross profit, but that ignores all the other expenses the company incurred. Further, the $45,000 in dividends came from the Claimant Corporation's current income (retained earnings the year before were almost zero), so the $45,000 cannot be counted twice, both as personal income and income in the Claimant Corporation available for support.
[32] As of December 31, 2023, the Claimant Corporation had $46,077 in its bank account and $39,075 in accounts receivable. Its major current liabilities were about $9,000 in withholding taxes owing to the CRA, and $23,902 owing to the claimant as a shareholder loan.
[33] The respondent submits that retained earnings in the Claimant Corporation are $263,203 for 2023, suggesting a much more financially healthy corporation. I do not agree with that analysis. The balance sheet reports the retained earnings as a total historical amount. Further, the respondent's analysis ignores the almost equally large dividends declared, also reported as a total historical amount. The general ledger shows that the actual retained earnings at the end of the year were $48,330 after $45,000 in dividends were declared and paid.
[34] As for expenses claimed in the Claimant Corporation, I have reviewed the 2023 general ledger. Bearing in mind the shifting onus of proof, I make the following findings. First, the total subcontracted services paid to the claimant's wife was $7,399. Considering the claimant's evidence of what she did, I find this is reasonable, and I do not add back any amount to the claimant's income for this expense.
[35] Second, the respondent challenges these expenses: (a) meals and entertainment; (b) vehicle; (c) telephone; (d) travel; and (e) personal insurance. Considering that the claimant works from home, his description of the expenses in his affidavits, my review of the general ledger descriptions, and that the claimant is agreeable to adding back some or all these expenses, I find that most of these items are personal, but that there is a small business component. I add back 100% of these, and the total is $12,920. This may appear not to allocate anything for the small business purpose; however, it does. I have not applied a gross-up to this add-back to account for the tax benefit to the claimant of the Claimant Corporation paying these expenses with pre-tax dollars. If these were not claimed as a business expense, they would have been paid from the claimant's after-tax income. In my view, this is a reasonable set-off which accounts for the small business purpose.
[36] Third, the respondent challenges the following expenses: (a) computer and internet; (b) office; and (c) office supplies. The total of these is $12,097. Considering the claimant's evidence and the general ledger, these expenses appear reasonable in amount and business related. I therefore do not add back these expenses.
[37] Finally, the respondent challenges the rent expense of $3,531. The general ledger account for the shareholder loans shows that this was 15% of rent, plus a rental deposit of $700. Given the respondent's evidence that he works from home, I find 15% is reasonable. The $700 is not allowed because: (a) it appears to be 100% and not 15%; and (b) it is primarily a refundable personal expense. I add back $700.
[38] In summary, I add back to the Claimant Corporation 2023 pre-tax income the sums of $12,920 and $700 for a total of $13,620.
[39] The claimant's 2024 line 15000 is $21,210 which includes $19,550 in taxable Canadian corporation dividends. Adjusting this in accordance with Schedule III of the Guidelines, the actual amount of the dividends was $17,000, so the adjusted line 15000 is $18,660.
[40] Turning to the Claimant Corporation's financial statements for 2024, it had a loss of $31,087. Again, the respondent's analysis refers to the gross profit, but that ignores all the other expenses incurred. The profit and loss statement shows subcontractor and employee wages to be $264,638 on revenue of $279,041, and before bad debt expense of $5,541. This is substantially greater total subcontractor and employee costs than the year before, on roughly equal revenue. The $17,000 in dividends came from the retained earnings from the year prior. The claimant repaid himself the shareholder loan owing to himself and withdrew $15,585 more from the Claimant Corporation, so he now owes the Claimant Corporation that amount.
[41] As of December 31, 2024, the Claimant Corporation had $10,733 in its bank account, and receivables of $751 for a total of $11,484. Major current liabilities were again to the CRA, with an increase to about $31,000 owing, being $8,000 in withholding taxes and $23,000 in employee remittances. The respondent states retained earnings were $358,320 at the end of 2024. Again, that is inaccurate because that is a cumulative total and ignores the cumulative total dividends paid. Retained earnings at the end of the year were $233. Essentially, the claimant took the remaining retained earnings and cash in the Claimant Corporation as dividends and a loan, while other liabilities (e.g. to the CRA) have not been paid and have increased.
[42] The Claimant Corporation's general ledger for 2024 was not produced. As a result, there is no corroborating documentary evidence of the claimant's evidence regarding the expenses. Considering what is proportionate, that the claimant is agreeable to certain categories of expenses being added back as personal expenses, that the amounts claimed for those expenses are relatively small and roughly consistent with amounts claimed in 2023, I use the 2023 findings as evidence of an accounting practice that can be applied to assess the 2024 smaller personal expenses that are claimed as business expenses.
[43] On the same reasoning as for 2023, I add back 100% of the following claimed expenses within the Claimant Corporation: (a) meals and entertainment; (b) telephone; (c) vehicle; and (d) travel. The total is $9,412. I do not add back expenses for computer and internet, office, and office supplies. In total, the costs are not markedly different from the year before, although allocated differently. I add back $3,799 claimed for rent expense, but deduct what I allowed for 2023, being $2,831. The net add back for rent is $968. The total add back to 2024 Claimant Corporation income for these smaller expenses is $9,412 plus $968 for a total of $10,380.
[44] The large increase in total employee and subcontractor expenses in 2024 requires a separate analysis. Based on the 2024 financial statements, the Claimant Corporation appears to have suffered a significant change in financial position and a loss. The revenues for 2024 were comparable to those in 2023. The biggest difference in profitability was the more than $100,000 increase in employee and subcontractor costs. The respondent states that the Claimant Corporation was initially doing well and then lost a major client and other contracts. He had to lay off staff. He has not produced the 2024 general ledger, nor has he produced a statement of payments to non-arms-length persons or entities, however he swears he did not receive a salary.
[45] The respondent interprets the increase in the total employee and subcontractor costs as the respondent doing no work and sub-contracting all the work and argues that income should be imputed to the claimant. However, in my view, that is speculation. Based on the claimant's evidence, I have no reason to conclude that the claimant is intentionally not working.
[46] The respondent also submits that because the claimant did not produce a statement of wages paid to the claimant's new spouse in 2024, the court should impute the new spouse's income to the claimant. I decline to do this for the following reasons. On the claimant's tax return, his spouse's income is stated to be $25,417 in 2024. A comparative income statement shows that $19,000 of this amount was dividends. However, this cannot have been from the Claimant Corporation because the claimant's new spouse is not shown as a shareholder, and the financial statements show that only $17,000 in dividends were paid, which were to the claimant. The remainder of the $25,417 was investment interest and $2,500 in "other" income. Even if the $2,500 in other income came from the Claimant Corporation, based on the claimant's evidence of the work his wife did, I find that is reasonable. Therefore, I do not impute income to the claimant on this basis.
[47] I have considered whether income should be imputed to the claimant because he has not produced the 2024 general ledger regarding the employee and subcontractor costs. For the following reasons, I conclude that should not be done. First, the claimant swears that he has not received a salary from the Corporation. In other words, the increase in employee and subcontractor costs is not actually payments to himself. Second, the claimant's evidence of the Claimant Corporation initially doing well, then suffering the loss of a major client and having to let go of staff, is plausible and consistent with the Claimant Corporation's financial statements, and the increase in the claimant's personal debt. Third, the respondent has produced an invoice from the CRA showing that as of August 2025, the Claimant Corporation owes the CRA over $55,000, primarily in wage deductions it has not remitted (about $49,000) and penalties. Fourth, the claimant has taken all the funds in the Corporation to pay the shareholder loan owing to him and borrowed from the Claimant Corporation. In summary, the financial statements and documents that have been produced support the claimant's evidence of a downturn in the financial health of the Claimant Corporation and his earnings.
[48] The claimant has an obligation to earn what he is capable of earning. I accept it was reasonable for him to try to become self-employed with the expectation that this will result in greater income and flexibility. I also accept that there may be growing pains and lesser income when a business is starting out. However, if the financial trend continues, the claimant cannot expect to persist in earning less than he can as an employee or sub-contractor for an employer, without having income imputed to him. The respondent submitted her research on average salaries of drafters and engineering drafters which are greater than what the claimant is earning now. That information was generated by artificial intelligence and is hearsay and is not admissible. However, I accept that in 2024, the claimant has earned less than he has in the past as an employee/sub-contractor.
[49] Considering all the above, especially that the claimant has taken all the funds in the Claimant Corporation, in my view, the fairest determination of his income for child support is to include all the pre-tax income of the Claimant Corporation in the claimant's Guidelines income, including the add backs for the smaller personal expenses. His line 15000 income therefore must be reduced by the dividends paid to him to avoid double counting. The dividends paid to him in 2023 and 2024 came from retained earnings that were created from income earned in the Claimant Corporation in 2023.
[50] Further, considering all the above and the fluctuations in the claimant's income, I also find that the fairest determination of the claimant's income available for support in 2023 and 2024 should be an average of his income. This is in effect what has taken place. The claimant took part of the Claimant Corporation income in 2023 and the remainder in 2024. The averaging will include the agreed income in the Consent Order (effectively for 2022) because that is some reflection of his capacity, the increase in that income in 2023, and the loss in 2024. Given what I accept was a good faith effort by the claimant to become self-employed, I will not impute greater income for 2023 and 2024, but the claimant should expect that if his income from self-employment through the Claimant Corporation does not improve, he may face an argument for imputation of income for 2025.
[51] A summary of the findings above are:
2022
2023
2024
Line 15000 without dividends
$187
$1,660
Pre-tax corporate income (debt)
$107,167
($31,087)
Personal expense add back
$13,620
$10,370
Total
$100,000 (agreed)
$120,974
($19,057)
Average
$201,917 /3 = $67,306
[52] I find the claimant's Guidelines income to be $67,306 for each of 2023 and 2024.
Respondent's Income
Parties' Positions
[53] The respondent accepts that pre-tax income of the Respondent Corporation could be included in her income, and if there is a personal component to a claimed business expense, that should be added back. However, she resists that some of the business expenses claimed are all personal.
[54] The claimant argues that the respondent has not made full financial disclosure. He challenges the same categories of expenses that the respondent challenged for the Claimant Corporation, arguing that they are personal expenses and not business expenses, and should be added back to the Respondent Corporation's income.
Analysis
[55] The respondent's income is less contentious as she is primarily an employee that receives T4 income, and her income has increased. The Respondent Corporation is a small part-time side business. The Respondent owned the Respondent Corporation 50/50 with a partner, but as of 2025, she took over the business. She recently obtained a new job that takes more time, and she plans to close the Respondent Corporation.
[56] For 2023, the respondent's line 15000 income was $54,813. This includes T4 income of 51,333 and $2,910 in investment and other income.
[57] The Respondent Corporation's tax year ends on June 30, and I have included the year ending June 30, 2023, in the 2023 Guidelines income determination. As of that date, the Respondent Corporation had $4,474 in cash and $1,275 in accounts receivable for a total of $5,749. It had $999 in trade account payables and $6,085 in "credit card loans". Shareholder loans payable were $5,311. The retained earnings were negative $6,646. For 2023, there was a net loss of $3,031 on revenue of $9,839.
[58] The respondent did not produce the general ledger for 2023 and 2024, but she did for 2025. I have done the same analysis for the respondent as I did for the claimant. Given the relatively small amounts, the positions of the parties, and what is proportionate, the 2025 general ledger has been used as some evidence of accounting practices to determine what is properly a business expense for other years.
[59] I find that the respondent has not proven the following are business as opposed to personal expenses: (a) meals and entertainment; (b) telephone; (c) vehicle. The total is $3,179. The adjusted net income is therefore negative $3,031 plus $3,179 = $148 net income. As the respondent is a 50% shareholder, only 50% of the income is attributed to her, being $74.
[60] For 2024, the respondent's line 15000 income was $78,969. This includes T4 income of $77,033 and $1,936 in investment and other income. This increase reflects her new job.
[61] Turning to the Respondent Corporation, as of June 30, 2024, it had $2,253 in cash and $697 in accounts receivable, for a total of $2,950. It had $6,470 in "credit card loans". Shareholder loans payable were $3,112. The retained earnings were a negative $6,775. There was a net loss of $126 on revenue of $11,590.
[62] I find that the respondent has not proven the following are business expenses: (a) meals and entertainment; (b) travel; and (c) automobile. The total is $3,790. There were no telephone expenses. The adjusted net income is therefore negative $126 plus $3,790 = $3,664. As the respondent is a 50% shareholder, only 50% of the income is attributed to her, being $1,832.
[63] I therefore find the respondent's Guidelines income to be:
2023
2024
Line 15000 income
$54,813
$78,969
50% Corporate income
$74
$1,832
Total
$54,887
$80,801
Issue 2: Child support amounts
[64] The Guidelines provide the framework for determining the amount of monthly basic child support payable. Child support has two components: the monthly basic amount, and s. 7 special and extraordinary expenses. Pursuant to s. 9 of the Guidelines, if there is shared parenting, the monthly amount payable must be determined by taking into account:
(a) the amounts set out in the applicable tables for each of the spouses;
(b) the increased costs of shared parenting time arrangements; and
(c) the conditions, means, needs and other circumstances of each spouse and of any child for whom support is sought.
[65] In Contino v. Leonelli-Contino, 2005 SCC 63, the Court discussed the Court must take a contextual approach to the analysis under s. 9. The Court must keep in mind the objectives of the Guidelines of a fair standard of support for the child and fair contributions from each parent, based on evidence. The Court must consider the "standard of living of the child in each household and the ability of each parent to absorb the costs required to maintain the appropriate standard of living in the circumstances": Contino at para. 68.
[66] Neither party argued that they had unusual increased costs of shared parenting, or that their circumstances, means, and needs are sufficiently different to not have a straight set-off of Guidelines table amounts. I therefore find that a straight set-off is appropriate.
[67] The order sought by the claimant is effectively a retroactive decrease in the amount of child support payable effective July 1, 2024. Although the respondent did not file her own notice of application, she argues based on her submissions on imputation of income, there should be a retroactive increase in child support. Neither party suggested that the court should not make a retroactive order, or raised the factors in Colucci v. Colucci, 2021 SCC 24 at para. 113, or D.B.S. v. S.R.G., 2006 SCC 37. Both parties requested that the court vary support retroactively and on the same calendar as in the Consent Order. In other words, their Guidelines income for 2023 will be the basis for child support from July 1, 2024, to June 30, 2025, and their Guidelines income for 2024 will be the basis for child support from July 1, 2025, to date, and ongoing until revised again by the parties after exchange of income information by May 31, 2026.
[68] I am satisfied that: (i) the claimant has established a past material change in circumstances; (ii) the decrease in income was not one of choice; and (iii) commencing in June 2024, the claimant advised the claimant that they had to adjust child support and that he was awaiting the financial statements from his accountant, and that by early September 2024 at the latest he had forwarded income information and his revised calculations for support to the respondent. The parties went back and forth for some time requesting details of certain aspects of the disclosure. The claimant made more timely disclosure than the respondent. I also find that a retroactive reduction will not be a hardship for the respondent, nor will it impact K. The respondent was able to contribute $40,000 to an RRSP last year.
[69] Based on the parties' Guidelines income, I retroactively vary the Consent Order and order that monthly child support was payable as follows:
a) July 1, 2024, to June 30, 2025: $639 per month payable by the claimant to the respondent, and $516 per month payable by the respondent to the claimant, for a net set off of $123 per month payable by the claimant to the respondent. The total amount payable to June 30, 2025, is $1,476 payable by the claimant to the respondent; and
b) July 1, 2025, to March 31, 2026, and ongoing: The child support tables were changed effective October 1, 2025, so two calculations must be made. For the period July 1 to September 30, 2025, $639 per month payable by the claimant to the respondent, and $773 per month payable by the respondent to the claimant, for a net set off of $134 per month payable by the respondent to the claimant. For the period October 1, 2025, to March 31, 2026, $632 per month payable by the claimant to the respondent, and $766 per month payable by the respondent to the claimant, also for a net set off of $134 per month payable by the respondent to the claimant. The total amount payable to March 31, 2026, is $1,206 payable by the respondent to the claimant.
[70] The net amount of the above is $270 payable by the claimant to the respondent. The parties agree that no arrears are owing up to June 30, 2024. The claimant did not pay any child support from July 2024 to March 2025. Since April 2025, the claimant has paid $120 per month child support, for a total of $1,200 to the end of January 2026. I have assumed that he made $120 payments on February 1 and March 1, 2026, for a total of $1,440. If I am wrong in this assumption, the parties can adjust the figures below accordingly.
[71] On the assumption above, I find that the claimant does not owe any arrears of child support as of March 31, 2026. He has made an overpayment of $1,440 less $270 = $1,170. This amount is owing by the respondent to the claimant. The respondent produced an FMEP account stating the claimant was in arrears of child support. That is no longer true based on the findings above. I trust the parties will be able to address this directly with the FMEP to have this adjusted.
Issue 3: Section 7 Expenses
Legal Framework
[72] The portions of s. 7 of the Guidelines which are relevant to the parties' arguments are:
7(1) In a child support order the court may, on either spouse's request provide for an amount to cover all or any portion of the following expenses, which expenses may be estimated, taking into account the necessity of the expense in relation to the child's best interests and the reasonableness of the expense in relation to the means of the spouses and those of the child and to the family's spending pattern prior to the separation:
(b) that portion of the medical and dental insurance premiums attributable to the child;
(c) health-related expenses that exceed insurance reimbursement by at least $100 annually, including orthodontic treatment, professional counselling provided by a psychologist, social worker, psychiatrist or any other person, physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech therapy and prescription drugs, hearing aids, glasses and contact lenses;
(f) extraordinary expenses for extracurricular activities.
[73] Section 7(1.1) defines the criteria for an expense to be extraordinary:
7(1.1) For the purposes of paragraphs (1)(d) and (f), the term extraordinary expenses means
(a) expenses that exceed those that the spouse requesting an amount for the extraordinary expenses can reasonably cover, taking into account that spouse's income and the amount that the spouse would receive under the applicable table or, where the court has determined that the table amount is inappropriate, the amount that the court has otherwise determined is appropriate; or
(b) where paragraph (a) is not applicable, expenses that the court considers are extraordinary taking into account
(i) the amount of the expense in relation to the income of the spouse requesting the amount, including the amount that the spouse would receive under the applicable table or, where the court has determined that the table amount is inappropriate, the amount that the court has otherwise determined is appropriate,
(ii) the nature and number of the educational programs and extracurricular activities,
(iii) any special needs and talents of the child or children,
(iv) the overall cost of the programs and activities, and
(v) any other similar factor that the court considers relevant.
[74] In Bodine-Shah v. Shah, 2014 BCCA 191 at para. 66, the court outlined the differences between special and extraordinary expenses:
[66] Special expenses listed in ss. 7(1)(a)-(c) and (e) are distinct from extraordinary expenses referred to in ss. 7(1)(d) and (f). Special expenses are defined as relating to child care, medical or dental insurance premiums, health-related costs, and post-secondary education. They must be found to be reasonable and necessary. Extraordinary expenses are not defined. Their extraordinariness is determined in the context of the combined income of the spouses, as well as other considerations, including the nature and amount of the individual expense, the nature and number of the activities, any special needs or talents of the child, and the overall cost of the activities. They also must be found to be reasonable and necessary. Relevant considerations for the tests of necessity and reasonableness include whether the expenses are necessary in relation to the child's best interests, and reasonable having regard to the means of the spouses, the child, and to the family's spending pattern prior to separation. See McLaughlin v. McLaughlin (1998), 57 B.C.L.R. (3d) 186 at paras. 81-82 (C.A.). In assessing "means", the court may consider the parties' capital assets, income distribution, debts, third-party resources, access costs, support obligations, receipt of support, and any other relevant factor: Delichte v. Rogers, 37 R.F.L. (7th) 81 at para. 38. Courts may consider whether the non-custodial parent was consulted regarding the expense, though a lack of consultation does not automatically preclude a finding that the expense is reasonable and necessary: Delichte at paras. 39, 44.
Consent Order
[75] The relevant terms of the Consent Order are:
3. BY CONSENT, the parental responsibilities of the guardians, to be exercised in the best interests of [K] are:
d) making decisions respecting [K's] education and participation in extracurricular activities, including the nature, extent, and location;
4. BY CONSENT, the parties are to consult each other with respect to the parental responsibilities in term 3 of this Order.
9. BY CONSENT, the party with parenting time will facilitate a phone call between [K] and the off-duty parent should [K] request a phone call with that parent.
Extracurricular Activities
41. BY CONSENT [K's] extracurricular activities will include karate, skating and swimming lessons.
42. BY CONSENT neither party will schedule [K] in an extracurricular activity during the other party's parenting time without first obtaining that party's consent, such consent not to be unreasonably withheld.
49. BY CONSENT, each party is responsible for registering [K] in at least one extracurricular activity per year.
55. BY CONSENT the parties will share the costs of special or extraordinary expenses in proportion to their respective incomes.
Parties' Positions
[76] There are four areas of contention. First, both parties allege that the other does not consult them concerning extracurricular activities for K or unreasonably refuses to agree, or is uncooperative, with respect to extracurricular activities the other proposes.
[77] Second, the respondent wishes to enroll K in club or competitive level sports, which are more costly than recreational level sports available through community centres, and which require a greater volunteer time commitment from parents. The claimant does not agree to the increased time commitment (part of which falls on his parenting time) and the increased cost of these activities. He is agreeable to recreational programs through community centres.
[78] Third, the parties claim reimbursement for expenses for which for various reasons the other objects to paying.
[79] Fourth, the respondent seeks a prospective order that a cell phone for K is a shareable s. 7 expense.
Analysis
[80] Both parties are looking for a solution for what they say is unreasonable conduct on the part of the other. However, there was no application to vary the terms of the Consent Order with respect to s. 7 expenses, so I decline to make such an order. I encourage the parties to follow the Consent Order, and consider what is in K's best interest.
[81] I find that the club level sports are an extraordinary activity, the cost of which is neither reasonable nor necessary for the claimant to share at this time. The claimant is in poor financial circumstances. He has another child with his second wife. There is no evidence that, at this young age, K has a special talent or skill that requires this level of training. K is already participating in a number of extracurricular activities. While the claimant consented to the 2024/25 hockey season, he did not consent to this activity again for the 2025/26 season. In my view, it is reasonable for K to be enrolled in recreational level programs at a community centre, but if the respondent wishes to place K in club level, then for the time being, until the claimant's financial position improves, she should bear the expense and time commitments.
[82] The following are the s. 7 expenses claimed by the claimant and the decision as to whether they will be shared:
a) Karate from July 2024 to December 2025 ($2,610). This is a s. 7 shareable expense per the Consent Order; and
b) Summer camps in 2024 ($598.41). The respondent agrees to this expense.
[83] The following are the s. 7 expenses claimed by the respondent and the decision as to whether they will be shared:
a) School cupcakes ($50.80); school supplies and t-shirt ($65.00); school field trip ($17.00); school ice cream truck ($3.75); backpack ($39.16); school swimming lessons ($12.50). These are not s. 7 expenses. Expenses such as school supplies, school sports fees, and school activities such as field trips are not extraordinary expenses. They are ordinary expenses and covered by monthly support payments. To be extraordinary, the participation must be beyond that of the average child: Clarke v. Clarke, 2014 BCSC 824 at paras. 47-58;
b) Dental and medical expenses above that covered by insurance (Total $369.43. Dental: $41.04 + $66.40. Medication: $25.00 + $228.62 + $8.37). These are shareable s. 7 expenses: s. 7(1)(c);
c) Private health insurance premiums from August 2024 to December 2025 ($1748.16). This is a shareable s. 7 expense: s. 7(1)(b);
d) Recreational swimming lessons ($59.30). This is a s. 7 shareable expense per the Consent Order;
e) Hockey fees and gear for the 2024/25 season. The claimant agreed to share this expense for this year only ($669.13 + $389.03 = $1,058.16). The claimant has paid $310 toward this expense and the respondent has paid $748.16; and
f) Hockey fees for the 2025/26 year ($1,264.29). The claimant did not agree to this, and it is not an expense he can afford at this time in addition to other s. 7 expenses. For the time being, it is not a shareable s. 7 expense.
[84] The total paid by the claimant that is shareable is $3,518.41. The total paid by the respondent that is shareable is $2,925.05. The average of the respondent's Guidelines income for 2023 and 2024, and the claimant's Guidelines income for 2023 and 2024 are nearly identical. Considering that these expenses span two years, and what is proportionate, the above expenses will be shared equally 50% each. The net amount owing by the respondent to the claimant is $297.
[85] The respondent seeks an order approving the purchase and funding of a cell phone for K as a s. 7 expense. She states she is being denied the ability to speak with K during the claimant's parenting time. She states K has never initiated a call to her when with the claimant, and when she tries to call, she has to escalate the request, it results in conflict, and she is given only a few minutes. In contrast, when K has parenting time with her, she gives her phone to K for him to frequently call the claimant. She denies interfering with the claimant's parenting time in an excessive manner.
[86] The claimant disputes the respondent's evidence and states that K is only away from either parent for two to three days at a time, and K is already free to call either parent during the other parent's parenting time using the other parent's phone. He is concerned the respondent will use a cell phone to interfere with his parenting time and call K excessively. He is also concerned regarding K's access to the internet on a cell phone.
[87] I decline to make this order because the respondent did not file an application to vary the Consent Order to prospectively order this is a shareable s. 7 expense. However, even if she had, the respondent has not proven that a cell phone is a reasonable and necessary extraordinary expense for the claimant to share at this time. K is only in Grade 2 and away from each parent a maximum of three days. By the Consent Order, if he wishes to call the other parent, it should be facilitated. I encourage the parties to follow the Consent Order, consider what is in K's best interests, and act reasonably. This includes respecting the other parent's parenting time, but also cooperating when the other parent reasonably wishes to speak to K. If the parties cannot cooperate, they might consider other agreements such as a set time once per parenting time to make a phone call.
Orders
[88] The claimant's Guidelines income for 2023 is $67,306, and for 2024 is $67,306.
[89] The respondent's Guidelines income for 2023 is $54,887, and for 2024 is $80,801.
[90] Based on the above Guidelines income, the Consent Order is retroactively varied as follows:
a) From July 1, 2024, to June 30, 2025, the set-off amount of child support payable by the respondent to the claimant is $123 per month; and
b) From July 1, 2025, to March 31, 2026, and continuing thereafter until further order of the court or agreement of the parties, the set-off amount of child support payable by the respondent to the claimant is $134 per month.
[91] Assuming monthly child support payments of $120 were made by the claimant to the respondent on February 1 and March 1, 2026, as of March 31, 2026, the claimant does not owe the respondent any arrears of child support, and the respondent owes the claimant an overpayment of child support of $1,170. The respondent shall pay this amount to the claimant within two months of the date of this order.
[92] Until further order of the court or agreement of the parties, the proportionate share of s. 7 expenses payable by each party is 50%.
[93] The net amount owing by the respondent to the claimant for s. 7 expenses as of December 31, 2025, is $297. The respondent shall pay this amount to the claimant within two months of the date of this order.
"Norell J."
